Multi-layered forest

[NO_MLFA_001]

Published

May 13, 2026

Recommended citation: Halbritter, A. H., Storaunet, K. O., Framstad, E., Jönsson, M., Snoksrud, O., and Jansson Asplund, U. 2026. Multi-layered forest (ID(s): NO_MLFA_001) v. 000.002. ecRxiv: https://github.com/NINAnor/ecRxiv/tree/main/indicators/NO_MLFA_001

TipMetadata
TipLog
  • 08 Nov. 2025 - Original PR
  • 13 May 2026 - ecRxiv template workflow; MLFA denominator includes NA as ensiktet; county/sub-county scaling (X0 = 0%, X100 varies by unit), truncation, split-county units; scaled regional/national bootstrap

1. Summary

  1. Background and rationale: This indicator represents the share of productive forest area with more than two vertical tree layers. In forests shaped by natural disturbances and succession, a substantial part of the forest will be multi-layered, although when stand-replacing disturbances occur at certain intervals, only one tree layer might be characteristic for the forest. Modern even-aged forestry practices will result in one tree layer for much of the managed forest. Multi-layered forests will generally have more varied habitats and resources for species than one-layered forests. This indicator has been proposed as a voluntary indicator in EU.

  2. Methods: Data for this indicator comes from the National Forest Inventory (NFI), where the number of tree layers around 0.1 ha of the NFI plot has been recorded for productive forest by current methods since the mid-1990s (Viken 2025).

  3. Interpretation: The non-scaled indicator is the area-weighted proportion of productive forest land that is categorized to multilayered (flersjiktet). The indicator is scaled for each county/sub-county with X0 = 0% multilayered forest and X100-values corresponding to the proportion expected under reference condition. Regional and national scaled values are area-weighted means of scaled county/sub-county values.

  4. Results and development needs: The indicator value for the current reporting period (2020-2024) is 0.91, and has increased since 2010 (2010-2014: 0.86, 2015-2019: 0.90). The lowest value is in the region Vest (0.75) and the higest value in the region South (0.96). There are no specific development needs for the indicator measure from the NFI data. However, a more thorough analysis and review of the assessment of reference values and the scaling function is needed.

1.2 Native language summary - Norwegian

  1. Bakgrunn og begrunnelse: Denne indikatoren representerer andelen av produktivt skogareal med mer enn to vertikale tresjikt (flersjiktet skog). I skoger formet av naturlige forstyrrelser og suksesjon, vil en betydelig andel av skogen være flersjiktet, mens etter forstyrrelser som gir høy dødelighet for trærne (intense branner, vindfellinger, etc.) vil ett tresjikt kunne være karakteristisk for skogen. Moderne skogbrukspraksis vil ofte resultere i bare ett tresjikt. Flersjiktede skoger vil ha større variasjon i strukturer og habitater enn ensjiktede. Denne indikatoren er foreslått som en frivillig indikator i EU.

  2. Metoder: Data for indikatoren kommer fra Landsskogtakseringen (LSK), hvor antall tresjikt rundt 0,1 hektar av LSK-prøveflatene er blitt registrert i produktiv skog siden midten av 1990-tallet (Viken 2025).

  3. Tolkning: Indikatoren måles som den arealvektede andelen av produktiv skog kategorisert som flersjiktet skog i LSKs data. Indikatoren skaleres for hvert fylke/delfylke med X0 = 0 % flersjiktet skog og X100-verdier som tilsvarer andelen forventet i referansetilstanden. Regionale og nasjonale skalerte verdier er arealvektede gjennomsnitt av skalerte verdier i fylker/delfylker.

  4. Resultater og utviklingsbehov: Indikatorverdien for nåværende rapporteringsperiode (2020–2024) er 0,91, og har økt siden 2010 (2010–2014: 0,86, 2015–2019: 0,90). Den laveste verdien er i region Vest (0,75) og den høyeste verdien i region Sør (0,96). Indikatoren er klar til bruk, men bør ikke brukes på finere oppløsning enn fylker. Det er ingen spesifikke utviklingsbehov for datagrunnlaget fra LSK-dataene. Fra 2024 registreres LSK-variabelen også i uproduktiv skog, slik at arealgrunnlaget for indikatoren kan utvides i framtiden. Imidlertid er det et klart behov for en grundigere analyse og gjennomgang av vurderingen av referanseverdier og skaleringsfunksjonen mellom X0 og X100.

2. About the underlying data

The number of tree layers is recorded for each 250 m2 National Forest Inventory (NFI) plot (Viken 2025). There are about 12 000 NFI plots in forest ecosystems, distributed on a regular grid, with varying grid density for forest below/above the coniferous forest line and in Finnmark county. The indicator value is an unbiased area-weighted estimate of the share of the productive forest area with more than two tree layers, for included NFI plots in any selected region. All NFI plots are sampled over a 5-year period, with a representative sampling scheme every year, allowing unbiased estimates from data for each year. For this indicator data from each full inventory cycle of 5 years are used, starting in 2010, meaning that results reported for 2024 are based on data from the years 2020-2024. The data are available upon request from NFI.

2.1 Spatial and temporal resolution and extent

Data are available from the mid-1990s, when current recording methods were established. Annual estimates are possible due to representative sampling every year. For this indicator all data from each full inventory cycle of 5 years are used, starting in 2010. The data are representative for all productive forest land within the selected region. Spatial resolution should be limited to the resolution allowing for reliable estimates, probably counties.

2.2 Sampling design

There are about 12 000 NFI plots in forest ecosystems, distributed on a regular grid, with varying grid density for forest below/above the coniferous forest line and in Finnmark county. All NFI plots are sampled over a 5-year period, with a representative sampling scheme every year, allowing unbiased estimates from data for each year.
The NFI-variable best_form is categorical and the multilayered category states that the forest has trees in more than two vertical layers, where tree-age variation also is a characteristic of the stand.

2.3 Original units

The indicator is specified as the area-weighted share of productive forest area (%) with more than two vertical tree layers (best_form = 3), relative to the total productive forest area.

2.4 Instructions for citing, using and accessing data

The data used for this indicator have been made available by the Norwegian National Forest Inventory under a specific data use agreement of 2025-10-20, between NIBIO and the specified users. Further use of these data requires a separate data user agreement with NIBIO.

3. Indicator properties

3.1 Ecosystem Condition Typology Class (ECT)

The multilayered forest indicator represent a structural attribute of the forests. Hence, this indicator represents a B2 Biotic structural characteristic.

3.2 Ecosystem condition characteristic

The vertical tree layers represent a structural attribute of forests. The indicator is characterised as Functionally important species and biophysical structures.

3.3 Collinearities with other indicators

There might be some positive correlations with indicators for old forest and dead wood.

3.4 Impact factors

Natural disturbances and succession at small scale or with moderate severity will generally result in multi-layered forests. Severe, stand-replacing disturbances may result in one-layered forests, but such disturbances are expected to be rare. Modern even-aged forestry practices will result in one tree layer for much of the managed forest.

3.5 Spatial aggregation

The spatial aggregation pathway is Aggregate (NFI plots to county) – [Scale – Truncate] – Aggregate (geographic region) – Aggregate (national level). For each NFI plot we derive weighted numerator and denominator areas from best_form, where multi-layered plots (best_form = 3) contribute to the numerator and all productive forest land (arealtype = 1) contribute to the denominator. Non-scaled indicator values for each reporting period are then calculated separately for counties, regions, and the national level as the area-weighted share: sum of numerator area divided by sum of denominator area. Scaling and truncation are applied after county/sub-county aggregation: each unit is normalised with X0 = 0 and a county/sub-county-specific X100 ?@sec-reference-levels using a linear function, and values above 1 are truncated to 1. Regional and national scaled values are area-weighted means of already scaled county/sub-county values. The indicator should not be used at finer spatial resolution than allows reliable estimates, probably counties.

4. Reference condition and levels

4.1 Reference condition

The reference condition is considered to be intact forest ecosystems, where effects of human activities are clearly subordinate to those of natural ecosystem processes and disturbances.

4.2 Reference levels

For the analyses below we use county/sub-county-specific reference levels: X0 = 0% (no multi-layered forest within the eligible denominator) and X100 (area percentage of forest being multilayered expected under reference conditions) varying among county/sub-county units. The lower reference value (X0) is set to 0%. For lack of better knowledge, the relationship between indicator values and ecosystem condition is assumed to be linear for values between X100 and X0. County values are scaled to 0–1 with ecTools::ec_normalise(); values above X100 are truncated to 1. Regional and national scaled point estimates are area-weighted means of scaled county/sub-county values.

4.2.1 Spatial resolution and validity

The reference value will be given as an overall aggregated value for the share of multi-layered productive forest for larger areas such as counties. Although indicator values from NFI plots may technically be aggregated at finer spatial units than counties, scaling indicator values at such finer spatial levels may result in biases.

5. Uncertainties

5.1 General uncertainties

Uncertainties relate to the variability in the frequency of forest layers among NFI plots and assessment errors for tree layers within NFI plots. This uncertainty is quantified by bootstrapping; the results report the resulting uncertainty for scaled values.

There will also be uncertainty related to the setting of the upper reference value and to the shape of the relationship between indicator values and ecosystem condition. These uncertainties will not be quantified.

5.2 Reported uncertainties

For non-scaled values, plots are resampled with replacement within each county and the ratio flersjiktet / (ensjiktet + tosjiktet + NA + flersjiktet) is recalculated in each replicate. For scaled county values, those bootstrap draws are transformed with the same linear normalisation used for point estimates. For scaled regional and national values, uncertainty is propagated using ecTools::ec_upscale() from scaled county bootstrap draws. The scaled tables in section 10 report the median and quantile intervals from these propagated distributions.

6. References

Viken, K. O. 2025. Landsskogtakseringens Feltinstruks — 2025. Vol. 11. 8. NIBIO Bok.

7. Import and prepare datasets

7.1 Dataset Multi-layered forest

The number of tree layers is recorded for each 250 m2 NFI inventory plot (Viken 2025). There are about 12 000 NFI plots in forest ecosystems, distributed on a regular grid, with varying grid density for forest below/above the coniferous forest line and in Finnmark county. The indicator value is an unbiased area-weighted estimate of the share of the productive forest area with more than two tree layers, for included NFI plots in any selected region. All NFI plots are sampled over a 5-year period, with a representative sampling scheme every year, allowing unbiased estimates from data for each year. For this indicator data from each full inventory cycle of 5 years are used, starting in 2010, meaning that results reported for 2024 are based on data from the years 2020-2024.

Load data 
lsk_data_file <- here("data/LSK_data_okologisk_tilstand_2026-06-03.csv")

lsk_data_raw <- read_csv(
  lsk_data_file,
  show_col_types = FALSE,
  locale = locale(encoding = "UTF-8")
) |>
  clean_names()

8. Spatial units

Aggregated indicator values are reported for counties, regions, and nationally. Where counties are split into parts in the NFI export, MLFA uses county/sub-county units matching the reference table in section 9.4. Aggregated indicator values should not be applied for spatial units finer than those that allow reliable estimates, probably counties or sub-counties.

9. Analyses

9.1 Prepare data and create derived variables

This section prepares the NFI plot data for calculating the indicator.

First we define the 5-year reporting periods that match the temporal structure of the indicator.

Code 
# Define multiple 5-year reporting periods starting from 2010
reporting_periods <- list(
  "2010-2014" = 2010:2014,
  "2015-2019" = 2015:2019,
  "2020-2024" = 2020:2024
)

Next we filter the raw dataset to include only productive forest plots (arealtype = 1), standardise the geography fields, and compute plot-level weights for the MLFA ratio. The indicator is based on the NFI variable best_form, where a value of "3" indicates multi-layered forest (more than two tree layers; flersjiktet). Missing best_form corresponds to height classes 1 and 2 and is treated as single-layer forest (enskiktet). Those plots stay in the dataset and count toward the denominator together with plots coded explicitly as enskiktet or tosjiktet (best_form "1" or "2"). The reported share is andel flersjiktet divided by (enskiktet + tosjiktet + NA + flersjiktet): the total productive forest area represented by multi-layered plots, divided by the total area of all plots that belong to one of those four classes (with NA counted as enskiktet). Plots with any other best_form code are left out of both the numerator and the denominator. Each plot contributes its sample area au_areal (hectares) to those totals.

Code 
lsk_data <- lsk_data_raw |>
  dplyr::filter(arealtype == 1) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    best_form_chr = as.character(best_form),
    best_form_eligible = is.na(best_form) |
      best_form_chr %in% c("1", "2", "3"),
    fylke_name_raw = region_fylke |>
      stringr::str_remove("^\\d+-\\d+\\s+") |>
      stringr::str_remove("^\\d+\\s*") |>
      stringr::str_squish(),
    county_part = assign_mlfa_county_part(fylke_name_raw),
    fylke_name = normalize_county_parts(fylke_name_raw),
    fylke_name_clean = stringi::stri_trans_general(
      stringr::str_squish(
        tolower(stringr::str_replace_all(fylke_name, "[0-9.-]", " "))
      ),
      "Latin-ASCII"
    ),
    region_code = assign_region_code(fylke_name_clean),
    region_name = assign_region_name(region_code),
    indicator_continuous = dplyr::if_else(
      best_form_eligible & best_form_chr %in% "3",
      1,
      0
    ),
    indicator_continuous_weighted = indicator_continuous * au_areal,
    mlfa_num_weighted = dplyr::if_else(
      best_form_eligible,
      as.numeric(best_form_chr %in% "3") * au_areal,
      0
    ),
    mlfa_den_weighted = dplyr::if_else(
      best_form_eligible,
      au_areal,
      0
    )
  )

Finally, we retain only the columns required for subsequent aggregation and reporting.

Code 
lsk_data <- lsk_data |>
  dplyr::select(
    sesong,
    au_areal,
    arealtype,
    region_code,
    region_name,
    county_part,
    fylke_name,
    fylke_name_clean,
    best_form,
    indicator_continuous,
    indicator_continuous_weighted,
    mlfa_num_weighted,
    mlfa_den_weighted
  )

9.2 Calculate non-scaled area-weighted indicator values

With the indicator-specific values we can produce area-weighted summaries for each reporting period at county, region, and national scales.

Code 
mlfa_results <- purrr::imap(
  reporting_periods,
  ~ calculate_mlfa_period_indicators(lsk_data, .y, .x)
) |>
  purrr::compact()

mlfa_results_county_all_periods <- purrr::map_dfr(mlfa_results, "county")
mlfa_results_region_all_periods <- purrr::map_dfr(mlfa_results, "region")
mlfa_results_national_all_periods <- purrr::map_dfr(mlfa_results, "national")

9.3 Bootstrap uncertainty estimation for non-scaled indicator values

Uncertainty in non-scaled values is quantified with county-level bootstrap draws. For each county and reporting period, plots are resampled with replacement 1000 times and the non-scaled ratio is recalculated in each replicate. The non-scaled unit is a proportion of productive forest area (0–1), also presented as percent (%).

Bootstrap draws per county (1000 per county per period) 
county_draws_long <- purrr::imap_dfr(mlfa_results, function(period_result, period_name) {
  county_meta <- period_result$county |>
    dplyr::select(county_part, fylke_name, total_area, region_code)

  purrr::imap_dfr(
    split(period_result$data, period_result$data$county_part),
    function(county_data, county_code) {
      boot <- bootstrap_mlfa_indicator(county_data, n_bootstrap = 1000)
      dplyr::tibble(
        period           = period_name,
        county_part      = county_code,
        sim_id           = seq_along(boot$draws),
        non_scaled_value = boot$draws
      )
    }
  ) |>
    dplyr::left_join(county_meta, by = "county_part")
})

The table below (Table 2) shows the direct area-weighted mean share of multi-layered forest per county for the latest period (2020-2024), with approximate 95% bootstrap confidence intervals (2.5th–97.5th percentile of 1000 draws).

9.4 Scale indicator values and aggregate to regional and national levels

County or sub-county reference values are created manually andlisted below.

County/sub-county reference values for MLFA scaling 
mlfa_reference_values <- dplyr::tibble(
  county_part = c(
    "Øs", "OA", "HeS", "HeN", "OpSØ", "OpNV", "BuSØ", "BuV", "Ve",
    "TeSØ", "TeNV", "AA", "VA", "Ro", "Ho", "SF", "MR", "ST", "NT",
    "NoS", "NoN", "Tr", "Fi"
  ),
  x100_pct = c(
    70, 70, 70, 60, 65, 55, 70, 55, 70, 70, 65, 65, 65, 60, 60,
    55, 60, 60, 65, 55, 45, 45, 40
  )
)

x0 <- 0

mlfa_reference_values |>
  knitr::kable()
county_part x100_pct
Øs 70
OA 70
HeS 70
HeN 60
OpSØ 65
OpNV 55
BuSØ 70
BuV 55
Ve 70
TeSØ 70
TeNV 65
AA 65
VA 65
Ro 60
Ho 60
SF 55
MR 60
ST 60
NT 65
NoS 55
NoN 45
Tr 45
Fi 40

Each county’s 1000 non-scaled draws from section 9.3 are scaled with ecTools::ec_normalise() (linear, X0 = 0, county-specific X100). Figure 1 shows the mapping for each sub-county.

Scale county draws; derive county uncertainty and feed ec_upscale 
county_draws_scaled <- county_draws_long |>
  dplyr::inner_join(mlfa_reference_values |> dplyr::select(county_part, x100), by = "county_part") |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    scaled_value = ec_normalise(
      non_scaled_value,
      x0 = x0,
      x100 = x100,
      fun = "linear"
    ),
    nation = "National"
  )

county_scaled_with_uncertainty <- county_draws_scaled |>
  dplyr::group_by(period, fylke_name, county_part, total_area, region_code) |>
  dplyr::summarise(
    median = median(scaled_value, na.rm = TRUE),
    p_025  = as.numeric(quantile(scaled_value, 0.025, na.rm = TRUE)),
    p_25   = as.numeric(quantile(scaled_value, 0.25, na.rm = TRUE)),
    p_75   = as.numeric(quantile(scaled_value, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE)),
    p_975  = as.numeric(quantile(scaled_value, 0.975, na.rm = TRUE)),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  dplyr::left_join(
    mlfa_results_county_all_periods |> dplyr::select(period, county_part, n_plots),
    by = c("period", "county_part")
  ) |>
  dplyr::mutate(region_name = assign_region_name(region_code))
Normalisation diagnostic figure 
norm_plot_data <- county_draws_scaled |>
  dplyr::group_by(county_part) |>
  dplyr::slice_sample(n = 100) |>
  dplyr::ungroup()

mlfa_norm_plot <- ec_norm_plot(norm_plot_data$non_scaled_value, norm_plot_data$scaled_value) +
  ggplot2::facet_wrap(~norm_plot_data$county_part, scales = "free_x") +
  ggplot2::labs(
    x = "MLFA share (proportion of productive forest area)",
    y = "Scaled indicator value"
  ) +
  ggplot2::theme_bw(base_size = 11)
mlfa_norm_plot$layers[[1]]$aes_params$size <- 2.5

suppressMessages(
  ggsave(
    filename = here::here("img/mlfa_norm_plot.png"),
    plot = mlfa_norm_plot,
    scale = 1.5
  )
)

mlfa_norm_plot

The scaled draws are then passed to ecTools::ec_upscale(), which propagates uncertainty through the spatial hierarchy. For each of 1000 Monte Carlo iterations, one draw is sampled per county and the area-weighted mean gives one regional value; a second ec_upscale() call repeats this from regions to national level.

ec_upscale county to region (compute once, save RDS) 
mlfa_mc_cache_deps <- c(
  here::here("data/LSK_data_okologisk_tilstand_2026-06-03.csv"),
  here::here("R/functions.R"),
  here::here("R/NO_MLFA_001.qmd")
)
mlfa_mc_rds_regional <- here::here("data/mlfa_mc_regional.rds")

if (
  file.exists(mlfa_mc_rds_regional) &&
  file.mtime(mlfa_mc_rds_regional) >= max(file.mtime(mlfa_mc_cache_deps), na.rm = TRUE)
) {
  mlfa_mc_regional <- readRDS(mlfa_mc_rds_regional)
} else {
  mlfa_mc_regional <- ecTools::ec_upscale(
    data        = county_draws_scaled,
    variable    = scaled_value,
    weight      = total_area,
    start_units = county_part,
    end_units   = region_code,
    year        = period,
    end_units_name = "region_code",
    n = 1000
  )
  saveRDS(mlfa_mc_regional, mlfa_mc_rds_regional)
}
ec_upscale region to national (compute once, save RDS) 
region_area_weights <- county_draws_scaled |>
  dplyr::distinct(period, county_part, total_area, region_code) |>
  dplyr::group_by(period, region_code) |>
  dplyr::summarise(region_area = sum(total_area), .groups = "drop")

mlfa_mc_regional_for_national <- mlfa_mc_regional |>
  dplyr::left_join(region_area_weights, by = c("period", "region_code")) |>
  dplyr::mutate(nation = "National")

mlfa_mc_rds_national <- here::here("data/mlfa_mc_national.rds")

if (
  file.exists(mlfa_mc_rds_national) &&
  file.mtime(mlfa_mc_rds_national) >= max(
    c(file.mtime(mlfa_mc_cache_deps), file.mtime(mlfa_mc_rds_regional)),
    na.rm = TRUE
  )
) {
  mlfa_mc_national <- readRDS(mlfa_mc_rds_national)
} else {
  mlfa_mc_national <- ecTools::ec_upscale(
    data        = mlfa_mc_regional_for_national,
    variable    = sampled_mean,
    weight      = region_area,
    start_units = region_code,
    end_units   = nation,
    year        = period,
    end_units_name = "nation",
    n = 1000
  )
  saveRDS(mlfa_mc_national, mlfa_mc_rds_national)
}
Combine regional/national MC draws and build summary objects 
mlfa_mc_combined <- dplyr::bind_rows(
  mlfa_mc_regional |> dplyr::select(period, region = region_code, sampled_mean),
  mlfa_mc_national |> dplyr::select(period, region = nation, sampled_mean)
)

county_scaled_all_periods <- mlfa_results_county_all_periods |>
  dplyr::left_join(mlfa_reference_values |> dplyr::select(county_part, x100), by = "county_part") |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    scaled_value = ec_normalise(indicator_value, x0 = x0, x100 = x100, fun = "linear")
  )

region_scaled_agg_all_periods <- mlfa_mc_regional |>
  dplyr::group_by(period, region_code) |>
  dplyr::summarise(scaled_value = median(sampled_mean), .groups = "drop") |>
  dplyr::mutate(region_name = assign_region_name(region_code))

national_scaled_agg_all_periods <- mlfa_mc_national |>
  dplyr::group_by(period) |>
  dplyr::summarise(scaled_value = median(sampled_mean), .groups = "drop") |>
  dplyr::mutate(region = "National")

region_scaled_agg_with_uncertainty <- mlfa_mc_regional |>
  dplyr::group_by(period, region_code) |>
  dplyr::summarise(
    median = median(sampled_mean),
    p_025  = as.numeric(quantile(sampled_mean, 0.025)),
    p_25   = as.numeric(quantile(sampled_mean, 0.25)),
    p_75   = as.numeric(quantile(sampled_mean, 0.75)),
    p_975  = as.numeric(quantile(sampled_mean, 0.975)),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  dplyr::mutate(region_name = assign_region_name(region_code)) |>
  dplyr::left_join(
    mlfa_results_region_all_periods |> dplyr::select(period, region_code, total_area, n_plots),
    by = c("period", "region_code")
  )

national_scaled_agg_with_uncertainty <- mlfa_mc_national |>
  dplyr::group_by(period) |>
  dplyr::summarise(
    median = median(sampled_mean),
    p_025  = as.numeric(quantile(sampled_mean, 0.025)),
    p_25   = as.numeric(quantile(sampled_mean, 0.25)),
    p_75   = as.numeric(quantile(sampled_mean, 0.75)),
    p_975  = as.numeric(quantile(sampled_mean, 0.975)),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  dplyr::mutate(region = "National") |>
  dplyr::left_join(
    mlfa_results_national_all_periods |> dplyr::select(period, total_area, n_plots),
    by = "period"
  )

truncation_summary <- dplyr::bind_rows(
  county_scaled_all_periods |>
    dplyr::left_join(
      mlfa_reference_values |> dplyr::select(county_part, reference_value_pct = x100_pct),
      by = "county_part"
    ) |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      level = "County",
      exceeds_threshold = indicator_value > x100,
      original_value_pct = round(100 * indicator_value, 2),
      region_code = NA_character_,
      region_name = NA_character_
    ) |>
    dplyr::select(
      period, level, region_code, region_name, fylke_name,
      reference_value_pct, exceeds_threshold, original_value_pct
    ),
  region_scaled_agg_all_periods |>
    dplyr::left_join(
      mlfa_results_region_all_periods |> dplyr::select(period, region_code, indicator_value),
      by = c("period", "region_code")
    ) |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      level = "Region",
      reference_value_pct = NA_real_,
      exceeds_threshold = scaled_value >= 1,
      original_value_pct = round(100 * indicator_value, 2),
      fylke_name = NA_character_
    ) |>
    dplyr::select(
      period, level, region_code, region_name, fylke_name,
      reference_value_pct, exceeds_threshold, original_value_pct
    ),
  national_scaled_agg_all_periods |>
    dplyr::left_join(
      mlfa_results_national_all_periods |> dplyr::select(period, indicator_value),
      by = "period"
    ) |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      level = "National",
      reference_value_pct = NA_real_,
      exceeds_threshold = scaled_value >= 1,
      original_value_pct = round(100 * indicator_value, 2),
      region_code = NA_character_,
      region_name = "National",
      fylke_name = NA_character_
    ) |>
    dplyr::select(
      period, level, region_code, region_name, fylke_name,
      reference_value_pct, exceeds_threshold, original_value_pct
    )
)

truncation_counts <- truncation_summary |>
  dplyr::group_by(period, level) |>
  dplyr::summarise(
    n_total = dplyr::n(),
    n_exceeding_reference = sum(exceeds_threshold, na.rm = TRUE),
    pct_exceeding_reference = round(100 * n_exceeding_reference / n_total, 1),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

truncated_values <- truncation_summary |>
  dplyr::filter(exceeds_threshold) |>
  dplyr::arrange(period, level, dplyr::desc(original_value_pct))

At county or sub-county level, truncation is evaluated by comparing the non-scaled point estimate to the matching X100. At regional and national level, the tables report cases where the ec_upscale() median equals 1. The following tables summarise how often truncation occurred in each period and list the affected units.

Period Level Total Exceeding reference % Exceeding reference
2010-2014 County 23 0 0
2010-2014 National 1 0 0
2010-2014 Region 5 0 0
2015-2019 County 23 0 0
2015-2019 National 1 0 0
2015-2019 Region 5 0 0
2020-2024 County 23 0 0
2020-2024 National 1 0 0
2020-2024 Region 5 0 0 

No values exceeded their reference and required truncation.

The time series below (Figure 2) summarises regional and national values over the three reporting periods.

Time series of indicator values by region and period 
mlfa_region_labels <- c(
  "1" = "Øst", "2" = "Sør", "3" = "Vest",
  "4" = "Midt", "5" = "Nord", "National" = "National"
)
mlfa_region_levels <- c("National", "Midt", "Nord", "Øst", "Sør", "Vest")
mlfa_region_cols <- setNames(
  MetBrewer::met.brewer("Archambault", n = length(mlfa_region_levels)),
  mlfa_region_levels
)

mlfa_plot_data <- mlfa_mc_combined |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    region_name = dplyr::recode(region, !!!mlfa_region_labels),
    series_type = dplyr::if_else(region == "National", "National", "Region")
  ) |>
  dplyr::group_by(period, region_name, series_type) |>
  dplyr::summarise(
    median = median(sampled_mean),
    p_025  = as.numeric(quantile(sampled_mean, 0.025)),
    p_975  = as.numeric(quantile(sampled_mean, 0.975)),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  dplyr::mutate(region_name = factor(region_name, levels = mlfa_region_levels))

ggplot2::ggplot(
  mlfa_plot_data,
  ggplot2::aes(x = period, y = median, colour = region_name, group = region_name)
) +
  ggplot2::geom_line(
    ggplot2::aes(linewidth = series_type),
    position = ggplot2::position_dodge(width = 0.18)
  ) +
  ggplot2::geom_pointrange(
    ggplot2::aes(ymin = p_025, ymax = p_975, shape = series_type),
    size = 0.8, linewidth = 0.45,
    position = ggplot2::position_dodge(width = 0.18)
  ) +
  ggplot2::scale_colour_manual(values = mlfa_region_cols, name = NULL) +
  ggplot2::scale_shape_manual(values = c(National = 19, Region = 17), name = NULL) +
  ggplot2::scale_linewidth_manual(values = c(National = 1, Region = 0.55), guide = "none") +
  ggplot2::scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), breaks = seq(0, 1, 0.2)) +
  ggplot2::theme_bw(base_size = 12) +
  ggplot2::theme(
    legend.position = "bottom",
    legend.box = "horizontal",
    panel.grid.minor = ggplot2::element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = ggplot2::element_blank()
  ) +
  ggplot2::guides(
    colour = ggplot2::guide_legend(nrow = 2, override.aes = list(size = 0.7, linewidth = 0.45)),
    shape = ggplot2::guide_legend(override.aes = list(size = 0.8, linewidth = 0.45))
  ) +
  ggplot2::labs(y = "Indicator value", x = "")

10. Results

The tables below report scaled values on a 0–1 condition scale. The indicator value is reported as the median, together with a 95% interval (2.5% and 97.5% quantiles) and a 50% interval (25% and 75% quantiles). County medians come from scaled bootstrap draws (section 9.3); regional and national medians come from ec_upscale() Monte Carlo draws saved in data/mlfa_mc_*.rds (recomputed when the data file, R/functions.R, or this document is newer than the RDS).

10.1 County-level results (scaled with uncertainty)

10.2 Regional results (scaled with uncertainty)

10.3 National results (scaled with uncertainty)

11. Export file

Export regional and national MC-propagated indicator draws 
write.csv(
  mlfa_mc_combined,
  here::here("data/results_NO_MLFA_001.csv"),
  row.names = FALSE
)

MeRIT attribution

This part supplements the CRediT statement with more granularity for method section.

Study design The study was designed by [AB, CD].

Data collection / fieldwork Field data were collected by [AB, EF] between [years]. Sampling protocol was developed by [CD].

Laboratory procedures DNA extraction was performed by [GH]. Sequencing was conducted by [IJ].

Data processing Raw data were curated and cleaned by [EF]. Code for preprocessing was written by [KL].

Statistical analysis All analyses were conducted by [CD] using [software], with verification by [AB].

Visualization Figures were produced by [KL], with input from [CD].

Reproducibility All code was reviewed and validated by [AB, CD].

TipSession Info

This information is mainly aimed at developers that need to recreate the R environment that produced the html. Important note: this information is about the R session that was running when the html was rendered, and if some of the code chunks were not evaluated when the html was rendered, they may have been executed in a different environment.
